cards.front: What special pole case is the first part of Lesson 12 about?
cards.back: It is about identifying one pair of poles on the jω axis from a special Routh-table pattern.

cards.front: What symbol does the note use when a zero appears during Routh construction?
cards.back: It replaces the zero by a small epsilon so the sign behavior can still be studied.

cards.front: Which row is highlighted in the note's jω-axis condition?
cards.back: The row indexed by s^1 is highlighted. A zero there, with the rest of the first column positive, signals one pair on the jω axis.

cards.front: Why is a zero in the Routh table important?
cards.back: Because it may indicate a boundary case rather than an ordinary stable or unstable pattern.

cards.front: What does the first column of the Routh table tell you?
cards.back: It tells you the stability sign pattern and can also reveal special cases like poles on the jω axis.

cards.front: What conclusion does the note draw when the s^1-row first-column element is zero but the others are positive?
cards.back: It concludes that one pair of poles lies on the jω axis.

cards.front: What pair is obtained in the worked jω-axis example?
cards.back: The note identifies the pair as ±j√2.

cards.front: Why should you not call the system unstable as soon as a zero appears in the table?
cards.back: Because the zero may instead correspond to a special boundary case, and the lesson shows how to interpret that case correctly.

cards.front: What is the role of epsilon in Example 3?
cards.back: It lets the table continue so you can examine how the sign pattern behaves around the zero entry.

cards.front: What exam habit does this concept teach?
cards.back: Always inspect the first column carefully and explain the meaning of any zero instead of treating it like routine arithmetic noise.
